title: Howl's Moving Castle - Chapter Seven: In which a scarecrow prevents Sophie from leaving the castle (Fragment 4)
text:
“Let’s get this straight,” Sophie said dizzily. “We are talking about the Lettie Hatter who works in Cesari’s pastry shop, are we?”

“Of course we are!” Michael said jubilantly. “I’ve loved her ever since she started work there, and I almost couldn’t believe it when she said she loved me. She has hundreds of admirers. I wouldn’t have been surprised if Howl was one of them. I’m so relieved ! I got you a cake from Cesari’s to celebrate. Where did I put it? Oh, here it is.”

He thrust the pink-and-white box at Sophie. Onion fell off it into her lap.

“How old are you, my child?” Sophie asked.

“Fifteen last May Day,” said Michael. “Calcifer sent fireworks up from the castle. Didn’t you, Calcifer? Oh, he’s asleep. You’re probably thinking I’m too young to be engaged—I’ve still got three years of my apprenticeship to run, and Lettie’s got even longer—but we promised one another, and we don’t mind waiting.”

Then Michael was about the right age for Martha, Sophie thought. And she knew by now he was a nice, steady lad with a career as a wizard ahead of him. Bless Martha’s heart! When she thought back to that bewildering May Day, she realized that Michael had been one of that shouting group leaning on the counter in front of Martha. But Howl had been outside in Market Square.

“Are you sure your Lettie was telling the truth about Howl?” she asked anxiously.

“Positive,” said Michael. “I know when she’s lying. She stops twiddling her thumbs.”

“She does too!” said Sophie, chuckling.

“How do you know?” Michael asked in surprise.

“Because she’s my sis-ter—er—sister’s granddaughter,” said Sophie, “and as a small girl she was not always terribly truthful. But she’s quite young and—er … Well, suppose she changes as she grows. She—er—may not look quite the same in a year or so.”

“Neither will I,” said Michael. “People our age change all the time. It won’t worry us. She’ll still be Lettie.”

In a manner of speaking, Sophie thought. “But suppose she was telling the truth,” she went on anxiously, “and she just knew Howl under a false name?”

“Don’t worry, I thought of that!” said Michael. “I described Howl—you must admit he’s pretty recognizable—and she really hadn’t seen him or his wretched guitar. I didn’t even have to tell her he doesn’t know how to play the thing. She never set eyes on him, and she twiddled her thumbs all the time she said she hadn’t.”

“That’s a relief!” Sophie said, lying stiffly back in her chair. And it certainly was a relief about Martha. But it was not much of a relief, because Sophie was positive that the only other Lettie Hatter in the district was the real one. If there had been another, someone would have come into the hat shop and gossiped about it. It sounded like strong-minded Lettie, not giving in to Howl. What worried Sophie was that Lettie had told Howl her real name. She might not be sure about him, but she liked him enough to trust him with an important secret like that.

“Don’t look so anxious!” Michael laughed, leaning on the back of the chair. “Have a look at the cake I brought you.”

As Sophie started opening the box, it dawned on her that Michael had gone from seeing her as a natural disaster to actually liking her. She was so pleased and grateful that she decided to tell Michael the whole truth about Lettie and Martha and herself too. It was only fair to let him know the sort of family he meant to marry into. The box came open. It was Cesari’s most luscious cake, covered in cream and cherries and little curls of chocolate. “Oh!” said Sophie.

meta.author: Diana Wynne Jones
meta.sectionTitle: Chapter Seven: In which a scarecrow prevents Sophie from leaving the castle

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows worry or concern about an uncertain outcome.
vocabulary.example: “Are you sure your Lettie was telling the truth about Howl?” she asked anxiously.

vocabulary.definition: The position of someone who works for an expert to learn a trade or profession.
vocabulary.example: I’ve still got three years of my apprenticeship to run.

vocabulary.definition: Causing confusion or puzzlement.
vocabulary.example: When she thought back to that bewildering May Day...

vocabulary.definition: In a way that makes one feel unsteady or faint; confused or bewildered.
vocabulary.example: “Let’s get this straight,” Sophie said dizzily.

vocabulary.definition: Agreed to marry someone.
vocabulary.example: You’re probably thinking I’m too young to be engaged.

vocabulary.definition: Feeling or showing thanks; appreciative.
vocabulary.example: She was so pleased and grateful that she decided to tell Michael the whole truth.

vocabulary.definition: A stringed musical instrument with a fretted fingerboard, typically played by strumming or plucking.
vocabulary.example: She never set eyes on him, and she twiddled her thumbs all the time she said she hadn’t.

vocabulary.definition: In a way that shows great happiness or triumph.
vocabulary.example: “Of course we are!” Michael said jubilantly.

vocabulary.definition: To rest for support on something or someone.
vocabulary.example: Michael laughed, leaning on the back of the chair.

vocabulary.definition: Highly pleasing to the taste or smell; richly satisfying.
vocabulary.example: It was Cesari’s most luscious cake.

vocabulary.definition: An expression used to indicate that a statement is not meant literally but figuratively.
vocabulary.example: In a manner of speaking, Sophie thought.

vocabulary.definition: Having a serious, important, or useful quality or purpose.
vocabulary.example: Meaningful multi-word expressions and collocations.

vocabulary.definition: A combination of multiple words that function as a single unit of meaning.
vocabulary.example: Meaningful multi-word expressions and collocations.

vocabulary.definition: A sudden event such as a flood or earthquake that causes great damage or loss of life; metaphorically used for a person causing trouble.
vocabulary.example: Michael had gone from seeing her as a natural disaster to actually liking her.

vocabulary.definition: A dough of flour, water, and shortening used as a base and covering in baked dishes.
vocabulary.example: Lettie Hatter who works in Cesari’s pastry shop.

vocabulary.definition: Able to be identified or known from previous encounters or knowledge.
vocabulary.example: You must admit he’s pretty recognizable.

vocabulary.definition: The alleviation of pain, distress, or anxiety.
vocabulary.example: That’s a relief!

vocabulary.definition: No longer feeling distressed or anxious; comforted.
vocabulary.example: I’m so relieved! I got you a cake from Cesari’s to celebrate.

vocabulary.definition: A group of people loudly calling out or speaking.
vocabulary.example: Michael had been one of that shouting group leaning on the counter.

vocabulary.definition: Firmly fixed, constant, not changing or fluctuating.
vocabulary.example: He was a nice, steady lad with a career as a wizard ahead of him.

vocabulary.definition: In a formal, rigid, or unnatural manner.
vocabulary.example: Sophie said, lying stiffly back in her chair.

vocabulary.definition: Telling or expressing the truth; honest.
vocabulary.example: As a small girl she was not always terribly truthful.

vocabulary.definition: To be bored or to wait idly, doing nothing.
vocabulary.example: She stops twiddling her thumbs.

vocabulary.definition: To rotate or spin something quickly between fingers; to fiddle.
vocabulary.example: She stops twiddling her thumbs.
